question: Define the distance between two non-empty subsets A and B of a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: The distance d(A, B) is defined as the infimum of the distances between points in A and points in B.
Formally: d(A, B) = inf { d(a, b) | a ∈ A, b ∈ B }.
This definition applies to any two non-empty sets, regardless of whether they are closed, open, bounded, or disjoint.

question: In a metric space (X, d), prove that if two disjoint subsets A and B are closed, and one of them is compact, then d(A, B) > 0.
expectedPoints: Assume, without loss of generality, that A is compact and B is closed.
Consider the function f: A → ℝ defined by f(a) = d(a, B) = inf { d(a, b) | b ∈ B }. This function is continuous.
Since A is compact, f attains its minimum on A. Let this minimum be δ = f(a₀) = d(a₀, B) for some a₀ ∈ A.
Because B is closed and a₀ ∉ B (as A and B are disjoint), d(a₀, B) > 0. Therefore, δ > 0.
Since d(A, B) = inf { d(a, B) | a ∈ A } = δ, it follows that d(A, B) = δ > 0.

question: Provide a counterexample to show that the distance between two disjoint closed sets in a metric space can be zero. Explain your counterexample in detail, including why both sets are closed, why they are disjoint, and why the distance between them is zero. Discuss the implications of this fact.
expectedPoints: Counterexample: In ℝ² with the Euclidean metric, define A = { (x, 0) | x ∈ ℝ } (the x-axis) and B = { (x, 1/x) | x > 0 } (the graph of 1/x for x>0).
Set A is closed because it is a standard closed set. Set B is closed because it is the graph of a continuous function on the open domain (0, ∞) and its complement is open.
The sets are disjoint because for any x > 0, 1/x ≠ 0, so a point (x, 1/x) is not on the x-axis.
The distance d(A, B) = 0. For any ε > 0, choose x > 2/ε. Then d((x,0), (x, 1/x)) = |1/x| < ε/2 < ε. Thus, points from the two sets can be made arbitrarily close.

Implication: This shows that the compactness condition in the related theorem is crucial. For two sets to have a positive distance, a stronger condition (like one set being compact) is necessary, as closedness alone is insufficient.
